segments.s613-g257: سپهر گراینده is a noun (سپهر) followed by a participial/attributive form (گراینده) meaning 'directing' or 'guiding' (the modifier follows the noun in this poetic ordering). یار تو باد is a predicative clause with یار as the predicate noun, تو marking the possessor ('your'), and باد as the optative particle ('may it be'); together it means 'May the ... be your companion/ally.'
segments.s613-g258: همان modifies داد ('that same/that very justice'). داد و پرهیز is a coordinated noun phrase ('justice and restraint/forbearance'), followed by کار تو ('your work/task') where کار is the predicative noun and تو is the possessor. باد again is the optative 'may it be', so the whole clause means 'May that justice and restraint be your work.'
segments.s613-g259: نهادند is the past 3rd‑person plural of نهادن 'to place/put' ('they placed'). بر is the preposition 'on/upon', followed by نامه‌بر 'the courier/letter‑carrier'. مهر شاه is a noun phrase 'the king's seal' (مهر with شاه as possessor). Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect; the basic sense is 'They placed the king's seal on the courier.'
segments.s613-g260: فرستاد is a past tense verb 'he/she/it sent' (subject is understood from context). برگشت and آمد are coordinated past verbs meaning 'returned' and 'came'; Persian often strings verbs without a conjunction, with و introducing the final verb here. به راه آمد is an idiomatic locative expression 'came on/upon the road' or 'arrived while on the road.'
segments.s613-g129: چنین گفت با خارزن شهریار is a fronted phrase: چنین 'thus' introduces reported speech, followed by گفت (past 'said') and a prepositional indirect object با خارزن شهریار 'to the kharzan, the king'. In classical Persian the word order 'چنین گفت + indirect object' is common to announce that what follows is a quotation.
segments.s613-g130: که‌گر is the archaic conditional particle (که گر = 'if'), followed by the noun phrase گوسفندش 'his sheep' and the negative-present 2nd-person form ندانی 'you do not know'. شمار is the noun 'number' and completes the object of 'know' (i.e. 'the number of his sheep').
segments.s613-g131: بدانی here functions as an exhortative/imperative 'know' or 'be aware' (classical usage), followed by the emphatic adverb همانا 'verily' and the interrogative/locative کجا 'where'. دارد is the present 3rd-person 'has/keeps', and اوی is the old third-person possessive 'his' — together 'Know, truly, where he keeps it.'
segments.s613-g132: شمارش is a noun 'his counting/treasury/one who keeps the accounts' (literal 'the counting of him'), بتو 'to you', گفت is the past 'told/said' (here introducing reported information — sense-list lacked a verb sense so note), and the clause کی یارد اوی 'who is his companion/helper' is a reported interrogative object: 'told you who his companion was.'
segments.s613-g133: چنین 'thus' again introduces a quotation; گفت is the past verb 'said' (reported speech marker). کای is a vocative 'O!', followed by the compound modifier رزم دیده 'battle‑seen' (a past‑participle adjective = 'seasoned in battle'), and the head noun سوار 'horseman/rider' — together vocatively addressing 'O battle‑seasoned horseman.'
segments.s613-g134: ازان 'from that' introduces the source; خواسته is a nominalized past participle 'that which is desired/asked' (i.e. 'his demand' or 'what he has taken'), کس with the following negative نداند yields 'no one knows' (کس here functions as 'no one' in negative contexts), and شمار (here 'the number/count') completes the idea: 'No one knows the number/from that demand.'
segments.s613-g135: بدان here functions as a prepositional/compound 'to that' (classical form; written here split as ب + دان), followed by خارزن 'the kharzan' (the recipient) and داد 'gave' as the past transitive verb. دینار چند is an object phrase 'a few dinars' (چند = 'several/a few' qualifying دینار). Word order is indirect object before verb, then direct object.
segments.s613-g136: بدو 'to him' is the indirect-object clitic/phrase, گفت 'said' introduces the quoted speech, کاکنون 'now' is an adverbial preface, شدی is the 2nd-person past/perfect 'you have become' (classical usage where شدی = 'you became' or 'you are become'), and ارجمند is the predicate adjective 'esteemed/esteemed one' — together: 'He said to him, "Now you have become esteemed."'
segments.s613-g41: ز اسپ اندر آمد is a classical/persuasive sequence meaning 'came off/from the horse' (ز + noun + اندر آمد). بدید آن سرای follows as a simple past clause 'he saw that house/dwelling' (بدید = 'saw', آن = 'that', سرای = 'dwelling/house'). Word order is literary: the prepositional/locative sequence precedes the verb, then the sighting is stated.
segments.s613-g42: جهاندار را marks the possessor/topic ('as for the sovereign'); را here functions like the classical object/dative marker that introduces the affected person. سست شد دست و پای is a predicative clause 'the hands and feet became weak' (سست = 'weak', شد = past 'became'); the possessed body-parts precede the verb in this inverted, emphatic order.
segments.s613-g43: This is an verbless descriptive clause typical of epic Persian: 'همه خانه' = 'the entire house' is followed by 'سرگین بد از گوسفند' = 'bad/dreadful dung from sheep' (implying 'was full of' or 'was fouled by'). The copula (was) is omitted but understood.
segments.s613-g44: یکی طاق = 'one arch' (یکی = one/a certain). بر پای means 'upon a base/foot' (بر + پای where پای = base/foot of something) and جای بلند = 'and a high place' describing its position; the phrase describes a single vaulted arch set on a pedestal and on high ground.
segments.s613-g45: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = to him; گفت = said — past). چیزی ز بهر نشست = 'something for the sake of a seat' or 'something to sit on' (ز بهر = 'for the purpose of', نشست used here as related to sitting/seat). The phrase expresses a request for a seat or seating provision.
segments.s613-g46: فراز آور is an imperative construction 'bring up / lift up' (فراز = up/aloft; آور = bring — imperative here). ای مرد مهمان‌پرست is a vocative 'O hospitable man' addressed to the addressee (ای + مرد + compound adjective مهمان‌پرست).
segments.s613-g47: چنین داد پاسخ = 'thus he gave an answer' (چنین = thus; داد = gave; پاسخ = answer). که introduces the quoted or subordinate clause that follows. بر میزبان indicates the target (at/to the host) — بر here functions like 'toward/at'.
segments.s613-g48: به خیره is an adverbial phrase 'in a stare / amazedly' (به + خیره). چرا خندی is a direct question 'why do you laugh?' with چرا (why) preceding the verb; خندی is a present-form 'you laugh' in classical/epic style. ای مرزبان is the vocative 'O marzban' addressing the border-warden/governor.
segments.s613-g89: بخور is an imperative meaning "eat." چند خواهی is a two-word clause: چند is an interrogative/adverbial "how much" and خواهی is 2nd-person present "you want" (literally “how much you want”); و بردار نیز links another imperative (بردار “take”) with نیز “also / as well.” The sequence gives two imperatives addressed to the addressee followed by an inclusory 'also'.
segments.s613-g90: This is a question: چه جویی is an inverted clause where چه = "what" and جویی is a present 2nd-person singular form of جستن/جوییدن ("you seek/are seeking"). ب + دین here functions as the prepositional phrase بدین (به + این) = "in/at this; to this." بی‌نوا خانه = "poor/destitute house" and چیز is the indefinite noun "thing"; together they mean "what thing are you seeking in this poor house?"
segments.s613-g91: همانا is an emphatic adverb meaning "verily/truly." بدیدی is the past form meaning "you saw" (second-person past). The sequence تو درویش مرد places the pronoun تو emphatically before the noun phrase; درویش مرد = "a dervish/poor man" (two nouns in apposition).
segments.s613-g92: ز پیری is a prepositional phrase "from/by old age" (ز = poetic 'from / because of'). فرومانده is a participial/adjectival past form meaning "fallen/overcome / brought low / downcast." از کارکرد is another prepositional phrase "from (his) work/effort" where کارکرد = "performance / work"; together they describe condition caused by age and toil.
segments.s613-g93: چنین داد پاسخ is a formulaic clause "(he) gave such an answer" (چنین = thus, داد = gave, پاسخ = answer). که‌گر is a fused conditional particle meaning "if" (که + گر); مهتری is a noun meaning "preeminence / superiority" (here: status/advantage). The clause introduces the conditional that follows.
segments.s613-g94: نداری here expresses lack (you do not have / there is no possession); in context it is read as "if you do not have (that superiority)." مکن is the prohibitive imperative "do not (do)." جنگ با لشکری is the object phrase "war / fight with an army/troops" (با = with; لشکری = soldiery/troops). The clause is an imperative prohibition conditioned by the previous if.
segments.s613-g95: This is reported speech introduction: چه نامی is the question "what (is your) name?" (چه = what). بدو means "to him" (be + 3rd-person pronoun), گفت is the verb 'said' introducing direct speech, and فرشیدورد is the speaker's proper name. (Note: some listed lemma senses do not include the exact verbal/noun senses used here; the grammatical roles are as described.)
segments.s613-g96: A string of negated nouns linked by نه ... و نه ...: نه بوم و نه پوشش (neither home/abode nor covering/cloak), نه خواب (nor sleep) و نه خورد (nor food/eating). Each noun is coordinated by و and negated by نه; this lists the speaker's lack of shelter, clothing, rest, and sustenance.
segments.s613-g225: نسازیم is the 1st-person-plural present/subjunctive form with the negative prefix ن- (literally “let us not / we do not make”); it serves as the main verb. ازان = از آن (“from that/of that”) is a prepositional/genitive phrase referring back to what was just mentioned. رنج is the noun object “suffering/hardship,” and بنیاد گنج is a noun phrase where بنیاد (head, “foundation”) takes گنج (“treasure”) as its genitive attribute (foundation of a treasure).
segments.s613-g226: نبندیم is the 1st-person-plural negative form of بستن (“let us not bind / we will not close”), functioning as the second main verb. دل is the direct object “heart.” در introduces a locative phrase “in,” and سرای سپنج is a noun phrase meaning literally “the house/room of the sponge” (سرای head + سپنج modifier) used here figuratively; note the poet places the locative phrase after the verb, following flexible classical Persian order.
segments.s613-g227: فریدون is the subject (proper name). نه is the negation particle placed before the predicate. پیداست is a compound of پیداست (پیدا + است) meaning “is found/visible”; together the phrase means “Fereydun is not present/visible.” اندر جهان is a locative phrase “in the world,” following the verb phrase.
segments.s613-g228: همان (= “that same / the very same”) picks out the individuals to follow. ایرج و سلم و تور is a simple coordination of three names (Irāj, Salm, Tūr). از introduces a relation of source/class (“of/from”), and مهان here refers to the eminent / great ones (a poetic collective), so the phrase means roughly “those same Irāj, Salm and Tur among the great.”
segments.s613-g229: همان again means “that same / those same.” جم و کاوس is a coordinated pair of proper names (Jam and Kay Kavus). با functions as a preposition “with/along with,” and کیقباد is another proper name added by that preposition (’with Kay Qobad’). The sequence lists famous rulers together.
segments.s613-g230: جزین = جز این (“apart from these / except these”) introduces an exclusion. نامداران is a plural noun “renowned ones / illustrious persons.” که introduces a relative clause; داریم یاد literally “we have remembrance” (یاد داشتن = to remember), so the clause که داریم یاد = “whom we remember” or “of whom we keep memory.”
segments.s613-g231: پدرم = “my father” (پدر + possessive suffix). آنک is a relative particle “who/that,” introducing a relative clause. ز / زو = از او (“from him/of him”) here combines with the relative to yield “whose.” دل پر از درد بود is a predicate “(his) heart was full of pain,” where پر از = “full of” and درد = “sorrow/affliction”; بود is the past copula “was.” The whole segment reads “my father, who had a heart full of sorrow.”
segments.s613-g232: نبد is an archaic/poetic negative copula (negated form of بودن, “was not”). It negates دادگر (“just/fair”), so نبد دادگر = “he was not just.” ناجوانمرد بود is a following predicate “(he) was unmanly / dishonourable,” with بود the affirmative copula for that adjective — the line therefore contrasts the negated quality with the affirmed reproach.
segments.s613-g105: بگفت is the classical past third-person ('he said'); in epic Persian the prefixed b- appears frequently but simply marks the past here. این is the demonstrative 'this' (object/what was said) placed before the verb. و links the two clauses. بگریست is another past third-person verb 'he wept'. چنان ... که is a result construction ('so ... that'); چنان = 'to that extent' and به زار is an adverbial phrase formed with به + adjective (زار, 'wretched/sorrowful') meaning 'in a wretched/sorrowful way'.
segments.s613-g106: که introduces the result clause of چنان ... که ('so ... that') — here functioning as 'that/so that'. بگریخت is past 'he fled'. ز is the poetic form of از meaning 'from'. آواز او = 'his voice' (آواز 'voice' + enclitic/pronoun او). شهریار (king) appears at the end as the grammatical subject of بگریخت, a common epic inversion where the verb precedes the explicit subject.
segments.s613-g107: بخندید is past 'laughed' — here with the transitive sense 'laughed at' (target marked by زان پیر). زان is the classical compound of ز + آن meaning 'at that / at that one' and introduces پیر 'the old man'. و connects verbs (and). آمد is past 'came' or 'set out' and به راه = 'onto the road/way' (به + noun marking direction).
segments.s613-g108: دمادم is an adverb meaning 'constantly/continually'. بیامد here functions as past 'came' (the b‑prefix is archaic/epic but marks the verbal form). پس in this context means 'after' (i.e. following him), so پس او سپاه = 'his army after him' (word order is inverted: سپاه او would be the neutral order; classical poetry often places modifiers/possessives in different order).
segments.s613-g109: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بیرون شد is a two-word expression 'went out/left' where شد is the classical past/auxiliary; together they mean 'he went out'. از marks origin 'from', and نامور شارستان = 'the renowned city' (نامور = famed, شارستان = city/town).
segments.s613-g110: به پیش اندر آمد is a classical phrase meaning 'came up before/appeared in front of (him)': به پیش ('to/in front of') + اندر آمد (literary 'came in/approached'). یکی is the indefinite 'a certain' and خارستان is the noun 'thorny thicket'. Note the archaic verb اندر آمد used instead of modern آمدن alone.
segments.s613-g111: تبر داشت is literally 'had an axe' (داشت = 'had/possessed'); the noun order (تبر داشت مردی) is slightly inverted compared with modern speech but common in verse: 'a man had an axe'. همی + کند gives a continuous or habitual present ('was cutting' / 'kept cutting') with کند the present stem 'does/makes', so همی کند خار = 'was cutting thorn(s)'.
segments.s613-g112: ز لشکر = 'from the army' using poetic ز for از. بشد here is an epic/historical past sense meaning 'it happened / he came about' or simply 'he came forward' (b- prefix and شد are common in Ferdowsi). پیش او شهریار = 'the king in front of him' (پیش او = 'before/near him' + شهریار 'the king'); again word order is inverted for poetic effect so the title appears at the end.
segments.s613-g241: دگر هرک چیزیش بود و بخورد is a compound clause: دگر هرک چیزیش ('all the other/whatever things of his') is the subject phrase where دگر ('other/remaining') precedes هرک چیز (an indeterminate/collective 'whatever thing(s)'); بود is the past copula/auxiliary 'was/there was'. و links to the next predicate, and بخورد is a perfective/derived verbal form meaning 'was eaten/consumed' (here used passively/intransitively of hisgoods). Word order follows older Persian: modifier (دگر) + determiner (هرک) + noun (چیزیش) + verb (بود/bخورد).
segments.s613-g242: کنون ماند با درد و با بادسرد is a short resultative clause: کنون 'now' adverbially frames the time; ماند is the past/stative verb 'remains/was left'; با introduces accompaniment ('with'); درد here names the suffering/affliction he is left with, and با بادسرد repeats the 'with' + instrumental noun 'cold wind' for emphasis. The repetition of با is a poetic device for parallelism.
segments.s613-g243: کسی را که نامست و دینار نیست begins with کسی را که ('someone who' or 'one who'), where را is the object/ topicalizing marker attached to کسی; که introduces the relative clause. نامست is an adjectival/nominal idea 'is without name / has no renown' and دینار نیست negates possession of money. The clause uses نیست to negate both predicates (parallel structure: نامست و دینار نیست = 'has no name and has no dinar').
segments.s613-g244: به بازارگانی کسش یار نیست is the main clause: به بازارگانی 'in/for commerce' (به marks the domain), کسش literally 'his person/anyone' functions as the object of possession ('he has no one'), یار 'companion/partner' is the noun complemented by نیست 'is not/does not exist'. The literal order is prepositional phrase + possessor phrase + predicate 'no companion exists'.
segments.s613-g245: دگر کودکانی که بینی یتیم opens with دگر کودکانی ('other children') where دگر is a distributive/contrastive modifier. که introduces a relative clause modifying کودکانی; بینی is the 2nd-person singular present subjunctive/indicative form 'you see' (classical/poetic usage), so the phrase reads 'children whom you see (to be) orphaned.' یتیم is the predicate adjective 'orphan.'
segments.s613-g246: پدر مرده و مانده بی زر و سیم is a nominal clause giving the situation: پدر مرده 'their father (is) dead' (مرده used as predicate/adjective), و مانده 'and (they are) left/remain' uses مانده to indicate remaining in that condition, بی 'without' governs زر 'gold/wealth' (and the omitted سیم 'silver'), producing 'left without gold and silver.' The verbs/adjectives are verbless copular descriptions typical of Persian.
segments.s613-g247: زنانی که بی‌شوی و بی‌پوشش‌اند: زنانی 'women' + که introduces the descriptive clause. بی‌شوی is a prefixed construction meaning 'without a husband' (بی- + شوی), and بی‌پوشش‌اند is a predicate 'they are uncovered/unprotected' (است omitted; ـاند is the 3rd plural copula). The coordinated adjectives describe their vulnerable status.
segments.s613-g248: که کاری ندانند و بی‌کوشش‌اند is a continuation: که 'who' introduces subordinate relative content; کاری here stands for 'a craft/work' (object) and ندانند is 3rd plural present negative 'they do not know' (classical form; دانند = they know), so 'they know no trade.' و links to بی‌کوشش‌اند 'and they are without industry/effort' (بی- prefix + adjective + ـاند copula).
segments.s613-g121: This is two nominal states placed side by side with an implied copula: 'شکم گرسنه مانده' = 'the belly remained hungry' (مانده is a past/state participle meaning 'remained/was left') and 'تن برهنه' = 'the body (is) naked' (classical Persian often omits the verb 'to be'). Word order is noun + adjective + participle for the first clause and noun + adjective for the second.
segments.s613-g122: The correlative negative construction 'نه ... نه ...' (= 'neither ... nor ...') negates the following nouns. 'فرزند' = 'child'; 'خوی' with the enclitic 'ش' (his) = 'his kin/relatives'. 'نه‌بار و بنه' negates the paired phrase 'بار و بنه' meaning goods/baggage — here 'نه' excludes possessions. The conjunction 'و' simply links items.
segments.s613-g123: 'اگر' introduces a conditional ('if'). 'کشتمندش' is 'کشتمند' (killer/slayer) with the possessive suffix '-ش' = 'his slayer'. 'فروشد' is 3rd-person singular from 'فروختن' meaning 'sells' (here the protasis: 'if [he/one] sells'), and 'به زر' marks the price/means: 'for gold'.
segments.s613-g124: 'یکی' is the indefinite numeral 'one/a'. 'خانه بومش' = 'a house of his bōm' (بومش = بوم + suffix -ش, 'his homeland/abode'); 'کند' is the verb 'makes/would make' (result clause of the conditional), and 'پر گهر' is an adjectival phrase 'full (پر) of gems (گهر)', where پر precedes the noun it fills.
segments.s613-g125: 'شبانش' = 'his shepherd(s)' (شبان with possessive suffix). 'همی' is the old habitual/continuous particle ('would/used to' or 'always'), placed before the verb phrase. 'گوشت جوشد' literally 'meat boils' but functions as a cooking statement ('they boil/boiled meat'); 'به شیر' indicates the medium ('in/with milk') with به marking accompaniment or medium.
segments.s613-g126: 'خود او' is an emphatic subject 'he himself'. 'نان ارزن' = 'bread of millet' (نان modified by ارزن). 'خورد' is the past tense 'ate'. 'با پنیر' is a prepositional phrase 'with cheese' (با = 'with'). The clause therefore states the man's own plain meal contrasted with the shepherds' cooking.
segments.s613-g127: 'دو جامه' = 'two garments' (numeral + noun). 'ندیدست' is an archaic negative perfect/compound meaning 'has not seen / did not see' (here 'had never had'); 'هرگز' reinforces negation 'never'; 'به هم' = 'together' (به + هم), so the whole clause says he never possessed two garments at the same time.
segments.s613-g128: 'ازویست' is a fused classical phrase (از وی است) functioning as a causal/linking particle 'from this/therefore' or 'it is because of this'; 'هم' adds 'also'. The verbless clause 'بر تن او ستم' places the noun 'ستم' ('oppression/hardship') on the prepositional phrase 'بر تن او' ('upon his body') — the copula 'است' is omitted as usual in Persian, so: 'therefore hardship was upon his body as well.'
segments.s613-g97: بدو (to him) is the indirect-object/prepositional phrase placed before the verb; گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (classical simple past). بهرام is the subject (proper name). با کام خویش is a prepositional/adverbial phrase meaning 'to his own liking' or 'of his own accord' (با = with; کام = desire; خویش = his own), modifying how he spoke.
segments.s613-g98: چرا is the question word 'why'. نان نجویی combines noun + verbal stem: نان ('bread', idiomatically 'livelihood') and نجویی (second/third‑person (subjunctive/indicative) verbal form of جستن/جست‌وجو 'to seek') — together 'do you seek bread/your living'. بدین is the preposition+demonstrative 'ب' + 'دین' = 'by/with this' (classical spelling), followed by نام خویش 'your name' (نام = name, خویش = 'your own' with ش marking the possessive). The clause asks why he seeks livelihood under that particular name.
segments.s613-g99: کدیور is a proper name (the speaker). بدو is again the indirect-object 'to him'. گفت is the past tense verb 'said'. کز is a classical relative/prepositional particle 'کز' = 'that/from which' introducing the source 'from the Creator' (کردگار).
segments.s613-g100: سرآید is an optative/impersonal present form meaning 'may ... come' or 'may ... befall' (سرآمدن = to come/arrive). مگر here functions rhetorically as 'perhaps/may it be that' (classical usage). بر من = 'upon me' (بر = on/upon; من = me). این روزگار = 'this time/fate' (این = this; روزگار = time/fate) — the whole clause expresses a wish that this fate from God fall upon him.
segments.s613-g101: نیایش is the noun 'prayer/supplication'. کنَم (کنم) is the present-subjunctive/imperative-tinged form 'I should/do' used here as 'I will/shall pray' (classical present tense with volitional sense). پیش یزدان خویش is a prepositional phrase 'before my God' (پیش = before/in the presence of; یزدان = God; خویش = 'my own' with ش as possessive marker).
segments.s613-g102: ببینم is a first‑person present/subjunctive of دیدن 'to see' used here as 'I may see / I wonder whether I shall see'. مگر again carries a speculative/rhetorical sense 'perhaps'. بی‌تو = 'without you'. ویران خویش = 'myself ruined' (ویران = ruined/desolate; خویش + ش = my own / myself). The clause expresses hope or fear about becoming ruined in the beloved's absence.
segments.s613-g103: چرا 'why' introduces the question. آمدی is second‑person singular past 'you came'. در = 'in/into'. سرای here means 'house/abode' (classical noun). تهی = 'empty'/'devoid' describing the house. Word order is typical classical Persian: question word + verb + locative phrase.
segments.s613-g104: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'where/for that' (here 'where'). هرگز = 'never'. نبینی is second‑person present/subjunctive 'you will not see' or 'you do not see'. مهی و بهی are paired adjectives: مهی (literally 'moon‑like', poetic for beauty) and بهی ('good/excellent'); joined by و 'and'. The clause says you will never behold beauty or goodness there.
segments.s613-g153: This clause is a nominal predicate construction where two descriptive epithets precede the copula شد (became). دل‌افروز is a compound adjective ('heart‑pleasing') functioning as a predicate complement; گیتی افروز ('world‑illuminating') is a second epithet modifying the same subject. شد is the past/formative copula 'became'. The short word بد appears between the epithets in the manuscript reading; its function is not clear in modern usage (see senses mapping) and it likely reflects an archaic/poetic linking or a textual variant between the two epithets.
segments.s613-g154: چو here is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. آمد is the past tense 'came'. به درگاه is a prepositional phrase ('into/ to the court/presence') marking destination. پیروز شد is a compound of adjective 'victorious' plus the copula شد, i.e. 'he became victorious' — a result clause coordinated to the temporal clause introduced by چو.
segments.s613-g155: بیاورد is a narrative verb meaning 'he brought' or 'he led' (classical Persian simple past/present form used in epic narrative). لشکر is the direct object 'an army/host'. به کوه و به دشت are two parallel locative prepositional phrases ('into/onto the mountains and into the plains') indicating where the host was brought.
segments.s613-g156: همی is a classical/poetic adverb here giving emphasis or a continual/intensive nuance to the following clause (often used with past narrative verbs). گوسفند is the noun 'sheep' (subject/series). از عدد is a genitive phrase 'in (their) number / with regard to number' and برگذشت is a past verb meaning 'surpassed / passed beyond' (i.e. exceeded counting). Together the clause says the sheep outnumbered counting.
segments.s613-g157: This is an existential/copular clause: شتر (camels) is the topic/head noun, followed by بود 'was/were' as the copula. بر کوه gives the location 'on the mountain(s)'. ده کاروان is a numeral phrase modifying the quantity (ten caravans / ten per caravan depending on idiom); Persian word order can place the numeral after the noun it counts in older usage.
segments.s613-g158: به هر کاروان 'for/to each caravan' is a dative/prepositional phrase marking distribution. بر یکی uses بر in a distributive/assigning sense ('for each there was/assigned one'), and یکی ساروان states the assigned item/person 'one sarvan'. The whole segment assigns one sarvan (camel‑driver/caravan leader) to each caravan.
segments.s613-g159: This is a paired-list construction using the poetic preposition ز 'from'. ز گاوان introduces products taken from the cattle. ورز here appears as a nominalized product but its exact classical meaning is uncertain in the provided sense list (see senses mapping). The second coordinate repeats ز گاوان شیر explicitly 'and from the cattle, milk.' The structure lists different yields from the herds.
segments.s613-g160: A continuation of the inventory introduced with ز: ز پشم 'from wool' and ز روغن 'from oil' list material products, then ز کشت 'from the cultivation/crops' introduces agricultural produce, and پنیر 'cheese' closes the list as a product of those sources. The repeated preposition ز is typical of poetic enumeration.
segments.s613-g137: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb meaning 'he commanded/said'. تا introduces a purpose/subordinate clause ('that/so that') and is followed by the prepositional phrase از میان سپاه = 'from among the army' (از + location noun میان + noun سپاه). The verb-front order (verb then تا + clause) is common in literary Persian.
segments.s613-g138: بیاید is a subjunctive/jussive form meaning 'may/should come' (here: 'step forward') used after تا to express the commanded action. یکی مرد دانا is an indefinite noun phrase 'a/one wise man' (یکی = someone), and به راه ('onto the road' / 'forward') marks direction; the clause thus means 'that a wise man come forward'.
segments.s613-g139: کجا here functions as a poetic/discourse particle introducing the statement (classical usage rather than a literal 'where'). نام آن مرد = 'the name of that man' and بهرام بود is the predicate 'was Bahram' with بود as the past copula.
segments.s613-g140: سواری is a nominal predicate 'a rider/horseman', followed by the adjectives دلیر 'brave' and دلارام (here used as an epithet 'gentle/comforting'); و simply links the two adjectives. بود is the past copula 'was', asserting this description of the man.
segments.s613-g141: فرستاد is the simple past 'he sent'. با + noun phrase (با نامور سی سوار) indicates the force/squad sent: 'thirty renowned riders' (نامور qualifies سی سوار). Word order places the descriptive phrase after the verb.
segments.s613-g142: گزین کرده is a past participial construction 'having selected' / 'chosen' (گزین + کرده). شایسته modifies the following noun phrase مردان کار: 'men worthy of the work' or idiomatically 'men worthy of the task/deeds', so the whole phrase says these riders were 'selected as men fit for the work'.
segments.s613-g143: دبیری is the office/role 'secretaryship' (object), گزین کرد is the simple past 'chose/selected', and پرهیزگار is an adjective 'pious' describing the secretary. The construction means 'he chose a pious secretary'.
segments.s613-g144: بدان‌سان که 'in such a way that' introduces the restrictive clause. دانست + کردن forms 'knew how to do' (دانست = 'knew'), with کردن as the infinitival/light verb and شمار 'counting/accounts' the object: 'one who knew how to do/count the accounts' (i.e., able to keep the accounts).
segments.s613-g169: نخست is an adverb at the sentence start meaning 'first' and modifies the whole clause. آفرین کرد is a past-tense verbal phrase: آفرین here functions as an exclamation/noun 'praise' and کردن (کرد) is the past tense 'did' — together 'praised'. بر is the preposition introducing the beneficiary/target of the praise, and کردگار is the object 'the Creator/God'.
segments.s613-g170: که introduces a relative / explanatory clause ('who/which'). اویست is a poetic/contracted form corresponding to 'او هست' ('he is') — this classical contraction functions as the copula here. پیروز and پروردگار are predicate nouns joined by و forming the compound predicate 'victorious and Lord'.
segments.s613-g171: دگر here means 'again' / 'another time' and heads a parallel clause to the previous praise. آفرین بر شهنشاه کرد is the same structure as before: 'praised' (آفرین کردن) with بر + شهنشاه as the target, and کرد is the past-tense verb 'did/praised'.
segments.s613-g172: که introduces the content/reason clause. کیش بدی را is the definite direct object (را marks it): کیش 'creed/sect' modified by بدی 'evil'. نگونسار کرد is a compound verbal idea: نگونسار (mournful/woeful) is used predicatively or resultatively and کردن (کرد) supplies the past tense — 'made (it) woeful' or 'rendered it miserable'.
segments.s613-g173: چنین گفت is a compact poetic clause 'thus said' (چنین 'thus/so' modifies the verb). گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (here the lexeme's conversational sense is intended) and is followed by the vocative phrase کای شهریار جهان ('O king/sovereign of the world') addressing the addressee — note the poetic word order where the reporting verb appears before the vocative title.
segments.s613-g174: ز تو is a short prepositional phrase 'because of / from you' (ز = 'because of/from' in poetic register) with تو the pronoun. شاد is the predicate 'happy', modified by the adverb یکسر 'entirely/wholly'. کهان و مهان form a coordinated nominal phrase 'the world and the (great) ones/guests' — together they are the subject/affected group that is entirely joyful 'because of you'.
segments.s613-g175: کز is a relative/causal particle ('that/which' or 'because/from which') introducing the subordinate clause. اندازه دادت is 'the measure/portion that was given to you' (دادت = داد + ـت, past 'gave' with 2nd-person clitic). همی before the verb is an archaic continuous/emphatic particle and بگذرد is a third-person singular verb meaning 'passes/exceeds' (here 'will pass beyond' or 'will exceed').
segments.s613-g176: ازین is the compound preposition 'from this' (ین is the orthographic/poetic form of این). خامشی 'of this silence' is a genitive/attributive noun modifying گنج 'treasure'. کیفر specifies 'of punishment/retribution' as a further modifier; برد here functions as a verbal/nominal idea 'gain/profit' (in the sense 'is taken/gained'), giving the sense 'from this silence the treasure of punishment is gained'.
segments.s613-g1: بخفت is a classical/past form of خفتن with the archaic preverbal b- (past tense) meaning “slept.” The following temporal phrase آن شب و بامداد پگاه (“that night and at dawn”) specifies the time; the two nouns are joined by و for emphasis (night followed by dawn). The verb precedes the temporal phrase here because of poetic/inverted word order.
segments.s613-g2: بیامد is simple past “came.” سوی is a preposition “toward” and governs the following place phrase دشت نخچیرگاه, a compound meaning “the plain that is a hunting-ground.” Word order is Verb → preposition → place.
segments.s613-g3: همه راه و بی‌راه is an idiomatic pair meaning “every road and byway” (i.e., every possible route). لشکر is the subject 'the army' placed before the verb (a common Persian order), and گذشت is the past tense “passed” (or “moved on”).
segments.s613-g4: چنان شد که is a formulaic result clause “so/thus it came to pass that …” introducing the subordinate clause. یک ماه is a duration phrase “for one month” that precedes the verb ماند (past of ماندن 'to remain'), او is the third-person pronoun “he,” and به دشت marks location (into/in the plain).
segments.s613-g5: سراپرده و خیمه‌ها is a coordinated noun phrase “main/inner tent(s) and tents/pavilions.” ساختند is the third-person plural past “they built/erected.” The plural ending on خیمه‌ها and the plural verb make clear a collective action.
segments.s613-g6: ز is the poetic preposition “from.” نخچیر دشتی is a genitive/attributive sequence meaning “the prey/game of the plain” (noun + adjectival/attributive form دشتی). بپرداختند is a past plural verb meaning “they paid/disbursed” (here likely ‘distributed from the spoils’).
segments.s613-g7: The clause uses the negated experiential construction: کسی را خواب نیامد (literally “sleep did not come to anyone”). Here کسی را (with را marking the affected person) + نیامد (“did not come”) + بران دشت (“on that plain”) yields the meaning “no one on that plain slept.” Note the verb نیامد is past negative.
segments.s613-g8: This is an asyndetic list of things present: می‌و (poetic word for wine/fruit), گوشت نخچیر (“meat of the game/hunt”), and the instruments چنگ and رباب. The absence of a verb makes the list an implied accompaniment or inventory (i.e., there was wine, meat, harp and rebab).
segments.s613-g65: بدو is the dative/prepositional 'to him' (not the imperative 'run'); گفت‌گر is an archaic agent-noun formed from گفت (literally 'speaker'/'one who said') used here to introduce reported speech ('he said'); نیستت is the negative copula نیست with the enclitic second-person suffix -ت, i.e. 'you are not'; گوسفند is the noun 'sheep' used metaphorically ('a docile follower' / 'mere sheep').
segments.s613-g66: که introduces a relative clause 'that/which'; آمد is the past 'came'; به + خان تو is a prepositional phrase 'into/to your khan/inn/house' (به here = 'towards/into'); سرگین is the object 'dung/manure' placed before the verb, and فگند is an archaic past-form meaning 'threw/cast' (verb placed at the end as is normal in Persian clauses).
segments.s613-g67: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'; داد is the past 'gave' (here 'gave an answer'); پاسخ is the direct object 'answer'; که introduces the following result clause; شب تیره شد is a simple predication 'the night became dark' with تیره (adjective) before شد (the inchoative copula 'became').
segments.s613-g68: مرا (here split in the word list) is the dative/experiencer 'to me' (marked in the text by مرا/م + را), را is the object/definiteness marker, سر is the noun 'head' (the affected part), ز introduces cause 'from/because of', گفتار تو is 'your speech/words', and خیره شد is a compound inchoative/passive-like phrase 'became astonished/stunned'. The clause has an archaic word order with the dative/experiencer up front.
segments.s613-g69: یکی خانه بگزین is an imperative instruction 'choose one/a house' (یکی = 'a certain/one'); که here introduces purpose/result 'so that' (not a simple relative); یابی is a present-subjunctive/optative form 'you may find' (expressing the wished-for result), and پلاس is the object 'coarse blanket/blanket/rag' expected to be found.
segments.s613-g70: خداوند is the subject 'God' (or 'the Lord'); آن خانه 'that house' is topicalized; دارد is the present 'has/holds' used idiomatically with سپاس (praise) to mean 'holds in esteem' or 'praises'; سپاس is the noun 'praise/thanks' functioning as the complement ('God has praise for that house' = 'God esteems that house').
segments.s613-g71: چه here is interrogative/exclamatory ('why' / 'what for'); باشی is the subjunctive/optative 'be / remain' (rhetorical: 'why would you remain'); به نزدیکی is a prepositional phrase 'in/at the nearness of', and شوربخت is a substantive adjective 'the unfortunate/wretched one' (the person being near whom is being questioned).
segments.s613-g72: که introduces a relative clause modifying شوربخت ('who'); بستر here means 'bedding' (bedding/cover), کند is the habitual/present-like verb 'makes/does', شب is temporal 'at night', and ز برگ درخت is an ablative phrase 'from the leaves of a tree' giving the material of the bedding — the verb appears at the end as normal.
segments.s613-g201: This clause is V (verb) + object/order: the past verb برافگند (he sent/flung forth) follows the noun هیونی (the beasts/creatures or messengers) which is the direct object; پویان is an adjective/epithet 'moving/active' describing those creatures, and به راه is the prepositional phrase 'on the road/way'. The verb برافگند is an older/poetic past-form of برافکند (he sent forth) and the word order (N + verb + complement) is typical of Ferdowsi's epic style.
segments.s613-g202: بدان تا is a purposive sequence: بدَان (here functioning as a connective 'for that/with that purpose') + تا 'so that / in order that' introduces the purpose clause. برد نامه نزدیک شاه is the result/purpose: برد (carry/bring) governs نامه 'the letter' and نزدیک شاه is a prepositional phrase 'near the king'. In sense, this whole segment expresses purpose ('so that they may bring the letter close to the king').
segments.s613-g203: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آن نامه is the direct object 'that letter'. برخواند is the perfect/past narrative verb 'read aloud' (the prefix بر- + خواند), and it precedes the subject بهرام‌گور here in a topical poetic order (object + verb + subject) — common in classical Persian epic diction.
segments.s613-g204: This clause states the internal effect: به دلش 'into his heart' (به + دل + ـش, his) with اندر افتارد literally 'fell into' (افتاد in a causative/poetic past form) means 'struck/fell into his heart', and زان کار شور is an ablative/genitive phrase 'from that affair (زان کار) [came] شور' — شور meaning passion/ardor. Grammatically, the verb افتارد is used impersonally/experientially (something 'fell into his heart'), and زان links the effect to the preceding event.
segments.s613-g205: دژم گشت is an adjectival predicate 'he became sullen/grim' (گشت = became). The second half, دیده پر از آب کرد, uses a causative construction: دیده 'the eye' + پر از آب 'full of water' + کرد 'he made' — literally 'he made the eye full of water', i.e. 'his eyes filled with tears'. The sequence adjective + گشت followed by a causative clause is idiomatic in classical Persian narration.
segments.s613-g206: This parallel clause follows the same causative pattern: بروهای جنگی is a compound noun phrase 'the warlike / war-going ones' (بروهای = those who go / men who go; جنگی = warlike), and پر از تاب کرد is 'he made [them] full of تاب'. تاب here is poetic — 'vigor/brightness/endurance' — so the clause means he filled his fighting men with ardor or vigor. The verb کرد again is causative 'he caused/made'.
segments.s613-g207: بفرمود is an honorific/past narrative verb 'he commanded/said'. تا introduces the subordinate result/purpose 'so that' and پیش او شد دبیر is literally 'a scribe came before him' (شد here = came/happened, used as auxiliary/lexical verb). The word order (verb then تا + clause) reflects a command followed by its fulfillment.
segments.s613-g208: This short clause lists the scribe's requests: قلم خواست is 'he wanted/asked for a pen' (past tense خواست) and رومی و چینی حریر are coordinated nouns 'Roman and Chinese silk' — modifiers follow the nouns in a compact enumerative style typical of epic diction.
segments.s613-g25: یکی شارستان پیشش آمد به راه is a compact clause: یکی شارستان 'a certain town' is the subject, آمد is the past tense verb 'came/appeared'. پیشش is a pronominal adverb meaning 'before/in front of him' and follows the noun as a locative phrase; به راه is a prepositional phrase 'onto/along the road' modifying آمد.
segments.s613-g26: پر از + noun-list is a fixed expression 'full of ...'. از introduces the list of things the place is full of, and the nouns برزن، کوی، بازارگاه are coordinated by و. This whole phrase qualifies the town just mentioned.
segments.s613-g27: بفرمود is an honorific past-tense verb 'he commanded/said'. تا here introduces a subordinate clause meaning 'so that/that' (a reported-command or purpose clause). لشکرش is 'his army' (object/subject of the subordinate verb), با is the preposition 'with', and بنه ('small bundles') names what the army should set down.
segments.s613-g28: گذارند is a 3rd-person-plural present/subjunctive used after تا in reported command clauses: 'that they put/place/leave (their bundles)'. و joins the next clause. ماند is the past 3rd-singular 'remained'; خود او یک‌تنه places emphasis on the subject ('he himself') and یک‌تنه is an adverbial modifier 'alone / single‑handedly'.
segments.s613-g29: بپرسید is a past-tense verb 'he asked'. تا introduces the content/indirect-question clause that follows (functioning like 'that'/'to the effect that'). مهتر ده is a nominal phrase 'the village head' (مهتر 'chief' + ده 'village'), and کجاست is the embedded interrogative 'where is it' (کجا 'where' + ست copula 'is').
segments.s613-g30: سر اندر کشید is an idiomatic verbal phrase literally 'drew/withdrew his head inward' (سر 'head' + اندر 'into' + کشید 'drew'); it describes a small gesture or withdrawing motion before moving on. و connects the clauses; همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle ('then/so/indeed'); رفت is the past 'went' and راست is an adverb 'straight' modifying رفت.
segments.s613-g31: شکسته دری پهن و دراز is a noun phrase plus adjectives: شکسته 'broken' modifies دری (an older/poetic form for 'door' here). دید is the past tense 'he saw' taking that whole phrase as its object. The adjectives پهن 'wide' and دراز 'long' follow the noun and further describe the door.
segments.s613-g32: بیامد is past 'came'. خداوند here is an honorific 'the lord/master' (a human lord, not necessarily God). و joins the clauses; بردش is a contracted form 'برد + ـش' meaning 'took him' or 'led him' (object pronoun attached) and نماز is the noun 'prayer' — together 'led/took him to prayer' or 'led him in prayer'.
segments.s613-g217: 'همی بد که' is a set phrase meaning 'it is truly/very bad that' (همی here an intensifier). 'این مرد بد ناسپاس' is a noun phrase 'this bad (wicked) ungrateful man' (این = demonstrative + مرد noun, followed by two adjectival modifiers بد and ناسپاس). 'که' introduces the subordinate clause that follows.
segments.s613-g218: ز marks source/agent here ('of/from God'). نبودش is the negative past/existential with the enclitic -ش ('for him/it') and means 'he did not have' / 'there was not for him'. به دل در هراس literally 'fear in the heart' (به ... در is a locative phrasing: 'in his heart there was fear'); the whole segment says he lacked fear of God in his heart.
segments.s613-g219: یکی پاسبان بد is 'a certain/one bad guard' (یکی = 'a certain', پاسبان = 'guard', بد = 'bad'). برین خواسته should be read as بر این خواسته ('for/unto this, having desired it'): بر + ین (بر این) = 'for this', and خواسته is a participial/preterite form meaning 'wishing/desired'. Together it means 'a certain bad guard, having desired this...'.
segments.s613-g220: دل و جان = 'heart and life' (compound direct objects). ز indicates cause or source ('from/because of'). افزون شدن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'becoming/increasing'; شدن is the verbal auxiliary 'to become'. کاسته is the past/participial verb 'diminished/lessened' placed at the end (the verb referring back to the bad guard as subject). The phrase means he reduced/curtailed heart and life by blocking their increase.
segments.s613-g221: بدین دشت = به + این + دشت (poetic fusion) meaning 'in/at this plain' (بدین = 'in this/at this'). چه ... چه is the correlative 'whether ... or ...', so 'چه گرگ و چه گوسفند' = 'whether wolf or sheep' (both possibilities listed).
segments.s613-g222: چو introduces a temporal/conditional clause ('when' or 'if'). باشد is the present/subjunctive form of 'to be' ('are/is/when they are'). به پیکار = 'in battle' (به + پیکار), and ناسودمند is a predicate adjective meaning 'unprofitable/fruitless'; together: 'when they are in battle, (it is) fruitless.'
segments.s613-g223: به زیر زمین در is a locative phrase 'into/under the earth'—poetic layering of prepositions (به + زیر زمین + در) to mark subterranean location. چه ... چه again lists alternatives: 'چه گوهر چه سنگ' = 'whether gem (jewel) or stone'.
segments.s613-g224: کزو = از + که/از + او ('from which' / 'from it'). خورد here is used impersonally/passively in the sense 'is taken/obtained' or more broadly 'isfall/comes' (an older poetic usage). و connects the clauses. پوشش نیاید به چنگ literally 'a covering does not come to the claw/grasp'—نیاید = 'does not come/appear', به چنگ = 'to the grasp' (چنگ = claw/hand metaphor). The sense: nothing can be obtained from it and no covering can be seized.
segments.s613-g209: نخست is an adverbial fronting meaning 'first'/'initially'. آفرین here is a noun meaning 'praise' or 'acclamation' and combines with کردن (کرد) to form the verbal phrase آفرین کردن 'to praise/utter praise'. کرد is the simple past, 3rd person singular ('he did/made'). بر + noun marks the beneficiary/target ('to/for the Creator'). The clause order is adverb + object + verb + prepositional target, a poetic inversion of ordinary SOV.
segments.s613-g210: خداوند is a title here ('God, the Lord') in apposition to کردگار; پیروز is an epithet 'victorious' following the noun. و joins another target of praise. به introduces the additional object 'to/for' and روزگار is used as a quasi-divinity or abstract fate ('Fate/the times'). The whole segment functions as a coordination of targets of the prior verb 'آفرین کرد'.
segments.s613-g211: خداوند introduces an epithet/title ('the Lord'). دانایی and فرهی are a coordinated pair of nouns/adjectival epithets ('wisdom' and 'nobility' / 'culturedness') ascribed to that lord. The conjunction و links the two qualities; word order (title + qualities) is idiomatic in epic lists of attributes.
segments.s613-g212: The phrase repeats خداوند for rhetorical emphasis. دیهیم here qualifies the lord as 'of the land' / 'rural/territorial' (an older epithet) and شاهنشهی is the abstract noun 'kingship' or 'imperial rule'. Together they form another nominal epithet 'lord of the land and of imperial kingship'; there is no finite verb — this continues the list begun earlier.
segments.s613-g213: نبشت is the past tense 'he wrote/inscribed'. آن‌که‌گر is a fused archaic/Subordinate-conditional marker meaning 'if it were that / had it been that'. دادگر is a predicate noun 'just (one)/just ruler'. بودمی is an archaic first-person past form ('I had been' / 'I were') used here inside the counterfactual subordinate clause. The clause therefore expresses a contrary-to-fact condition.
segments.s613-g214: همین مرد را: همین 'this (same)' + مرد 'man' with را marking him as the (definite) object/topic. رنج is the object 'suffering/harm'. ننمودمی is the negative past first-person ('I did not cause/show') formed from نکردن/نمودن; here it means 'I would not have inflicted'. Together the clause is the apodosis of the counterfactual ('I would not have caused this man suffering').
segments.s613-g215: نیاورد is past negative 'did not bring/produce' (3rd person). گرد این is a poetic adverbial phrase 'about/around this' (گرد used adverbially). ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of' introducing causes, and دزدی و خون list 'theft and blood' (خون here = 'bloodshed'). The clause means 'this did not give rise to theft and bloodshed.'
segments.s613-g216: نبد is an older negative copular/existential 'was not/there was not'. هم adds the sense 'also/even' or simple emphasis. کسی 'anyone/someone' with را (object/topic marker) participates in an existential construction meaning 'there was no one'. به + بد رهنمون = 'to bad guidance' or 'as a bad guide' — رهنمون here is 'guide/guidance'. The clause thus reads 'nor was there anyone who served as a bad guide (to him)'.
segments.s613-g185: چنین خواسته is a nominal phrase meaning 'such/this desire' (چنین = 'thus/so', خواسته = noun 'desire'). گسترد is an archaic/poetic past verb 'گُسترد' (to spread) describing what that desire did; در جهان is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the world' that follows the verb for emphasis. Word order is compacted poetically: [demonstrative + noun] + verb + locative.
segments.s613-g186: This is a coordinated adjectival/nominal phrase describing the people affected: تهی‌دست 'impoverished' and پر غم 'full of grief' — غم is governed by the adjective پر. نشسته is the past participle/adjectival form 'sitting/settled' modifying those persons, and نهان is a final qualifier 'hidden' (adjectival/adverbial). The sequence places modifiers after the head in a poetic compressed style.
segments.s613-g187: به بیداد is a prepositional phrase 'to/under injustice' indicating the state or cause; ماند is the past verb 'remained/was left' and همی is a classical frequentive/continuous particle 'still/continually' modifying ماند. داد شاه ('داد' = 'justice', followed by the possessor 'شاه') functions as the subject/affected thing: 'the king's justice.' The poetic order places the prepositional phrase before the verb and the possessor after the noun without an explicit ezafe.
segments.s613-g188: منه here is an archaic prohibitive/negative command meaning 'do not' (classical مَنه). پند گفتار من is a genitival noun phrase 'the admonition/advice of my speech' (پند modifying گفتار, with من as possessor). بر گناه is a phrase 'with respect to/against sin' (بر = on/against). The whole clause is an imperative: 'Do not regard my counsel as for/forgiving sin' — functionally: 'do not take my words as condoning sin.'
segments.s613-g189: پی is a preposition 'after/following.' افگن is an adjective 'bright/gleaming' modifying the following noun; یکی گنج is 'a certain/one treasure.' زین in classical Persian often functions like 'از این' ('from this'/'of this') or as an attributive 'of these'; here it links the treasure to the preceding 'خواسته' (i.e., connected to this wish/occasion). خواسته again is 'wish/desire.' The phrase therefore means 'following this, a bright treasure (connected) to this wish.'
segments.s613-g190: سیوم سال را is a topical/definite time phrase 'the thirtieth year' (سیوم = ordinal 'thirtieth', سال = 'year'); را here marks the phrase as definite/emphatic. گردد is the classical present/subjunctive-like form of 'گردیدن/گشتن' used narratively as 'becomes/is turned' (i.e., 'was made' or 'became'), and آراسته is a past participle 'adorned.' Together: 'the thirtieth year became adorned' (i.e., was made splendid).
segments.s613-g191: دبیران داننده is a noun phrase 'the learned/knowledgeable secretaries' where داننده postmodifies دبیران. را is the definite object marker, and خواندم is first person singular past 'I called/summoned' (خواندن here means 'to summon/call' in administrative/poetic usage).
segments.s613-g192: برین is a compressed form of بر این 'upon this'; کوه آباد is a compound locative 'the inhabited/prosperous mountain' (کوه = mountain, آباد = inhabited/prosperous), and بنشاندم is the first person past 'I seated/placed/established' (بنشاندم = I installed/established). The verb takes the previously marked object (the secretaries) as its patient: 'I set them on this mountain.'
segments.s613-g161: This is a locative listing: items are given in a piled-up, emphatic order (همه دشت و کوه و بیابان) meaning “every plain, mountain, and desert.” کنام is a poetic locative/positional word (literally “bosom/side”) used here adverbially to mean roughly “throughout / in the midst of.” Word order is noun list → locative.
segments.s613-g162: This clause has the typical Persian SOV order with the object-marking را before the verb: کس (indefinite negative “no one”) + او را (“his/her” as definite object) + به گیتی (locative phrase “in the world”) + ندانست (past tense ‘did not know’) + نام (the thing not known, “name”). The verb ندانست is past tense; کس functions here as “no one.”
segments.s613-g163: سراسر همه is an emphatic expression meaning “throughout, entirely.” کنده is a past participle/adjectival verb meaning “torn up/dug up,” and سم (here ‘hoof’) names the instrument/agent doing the tearing; the line is resultive/agentive and roughly reads “the entire desert was torn up by hooves.”
segments.s613-g164: همان روغن گاو is a noun phrase “that same cow’s oil (ghee)” with گاو in a genitive/attributive relation to روغن. در سم is a locative phrase “in the hoof” (در + noun). به خم is a directional phrase “into/to a jar” (به + خم). The clause is elliptical (the verb is implied or carried over from context): the prepositional phrases indicate location/direction for the oil.
segments.s613-g165: ز شیراز وز ترف سیصدهراز lists sources/origins: ز + place (ز شیراز = “from Shiraz”) and then (poetically) “and from the delicacies (ترف) — three hundred thousand.” The token وز here is a poetic fused form (equivalent to و ز, “and from”) and the final numeral names the quantity. The sequence is paratactic, linking sources with the counted item.
segments.s613-g166: شتروار is an adverbial/qualifier meaning “in long file / in caravan formation.” بر لب جویبار is a locative phrase “on the brink/bank of the stream” (بر + لب + جویبار). بد here is a short poetic element whose exact literal sense is archaic/elliptical; it acts as an intensifier/qualifier in the clause and the overall order places adverbials before the implied verb or predicate.
segments.s613-g167: This uses a slightly inverted, poetic word order: یکی (a certain) + نامه (object) + بنوشت (past tense ‘wrote’) + بهرام هور (the person responsible). Here بنوشت is the past verb ‘wrote’ and بهرام هور is the subject (a proper name) placed after the verb for stylistic effect.
segments.s613-g168: به نزد means “to the presence of / to” (به + نزد); شهنشاه is the title “emperor” and is followed by the name apposition بهرام گور (Bahram Gor). The phrase as a whole is the indirect object/directional complement indicating the recipient of the letter.
segments.s613-g9: بیابان (fronted locative noun) sets the scene: "on the plain." همی is the classical/perfective-continuous particle here marking habitual/ongoing action in the narrative. آتش is the direct object "fire," and افروختند is the past plural verb (they kindled), so the clause literally reads: “On the plain they / were kindling fire.” Word order is literary: locative + aspect particle + object + past plural verb.
segments.s613-g10: تر و خشک coordinate two adjectives modifying هیزم (“firewood”): "wet and dry firewood." بسی is an adverb meaning a large amount/much and modifies the verb سوختند, which is past plural "they burned." The clause means that both wet and dry wood burned plentifully.
segments.s613-g11: برفتند is a past plural verb “they went/they departed” placed at the start (poetic inversion). بسیار مردم ز شهر is the following noun phrase: بسیار qualifies مردم as "many people," and ز شهر marks their origin "from the city." The clause therefore says that many people left the city.
segments.s613-g12: کسی کش functions as “someone (an agent/carter)”; here کش is an agentive element indicating a person who carries or bears something. ز دینار specifies what he bears/has ("of dinars/with dinars"), and بایست بهر = بایست + بهر: بایست is used as a lexical verb "stood" (past) and بهر "for/for the purpose of," so the phrase means roughly “someone bearing dinars stood there for [the purpose/need].” The exact nuance is idiomatic/literary—کش marks the person as a bearer of dinars.
segments.s613-g13: همی بود is the narrative copula with the continuous particle ("there was / there used to be"). چندی gives duration "for a while," and خرید و فروخت are nouns (or nominalized verbs) meaning "buying and selling"—together: "there was buying and selling for a while." The clause is an impersonal/state description.
segments.s613-g14: بیابان is again the locative/patient "the plain," ز لشکر uses ز + agent to indicate cause/agent "by/through the army," همی is the continuous particle, and برفروخت is a past verb here meaning "was inflamed/set on fire" (poetic use). So the clause says the plain was set alight because of the army.
segments.s613-g15: This is a parallel prepositional list introduced by ز ("from"): ز نخچیر دشت = "from the prey/game of the plain," و ز مرغان آب = "and from the birds, water." The noun phrases follow the preposition z (poetic order) and enumerate sources of provisions.
segments.s613-g16: همی یافته (یافت) is the past verb "found/obtained" with the continuous particle; خواهنده is a postverbal agent-like noun meaning "the one who desired/asked" (i.e., the seeker or guest), and چندان modifies کباب: "that much / so much kebab." Word order places the verb before its logical subject (common in poetry): "the seeker obtained so much kebab."
segments.s613-g193: This is a compact, inverted poetic clause: شمارش پدیدار نامد هنوز = “the tally/record had not yet appeared.” شمارش (the reckoning/list) is the topic; پدیدار functions as 'appeared/coming to light'; نامد here is an archaic past form (negative sense in context) meaning ‘did not come/appear’; هنوز is the final time‑adverb 'yet/still' modifying the negative past.
segments.s613-g194: نویسنده را پشت برگشت کوز = ‘the scribe returned with the jug at (on) his back.’ نویسنده is the definite person; را is the emphatic/definite object/topic marker used in older/poetic order (here marking the definite actor/participant). پشت + noun gives the locative 'at/on the back of…' and برگشت is the past verb 'returned/turned back.' کوز names the container carried on the back.
segments.s613-g195: چنین گفت گوینده کاندر زمین = 'Thus said the speaker in/therein the land.' چنین is the adverb 'thus/so'; گفت is the verb 'said' (poetic past) with an inverted order — the verb appears between adverb and subject; گوینده is the agent 'the speaker'; کاندر is a fused locative 'in/therein' pointing to the following noun زمین 'land/country.'
segments.s613-g196: ورا زر و گوهر فزونست زین = 'there gold and jewels were more abundant than here/this.' ورا here is locative 'there/thither'; زر و گوهر is a coordinated noun phrase 'gold and gems'; فزونست is an old present/past‑stative meaning 'were more/abounded'; the final زین is an archaic/deictic element (here functioning as a comparative or demonstrative) and is semantically opaque in modern Persian.
segments.s613-g197: برین کوهسارم دو دیده به راه = 'On this mountain (of mine) my two eyes were on the road.' برین is the fused preposition + demonstrative (بر + این) = 'on this'; کوهسارم is کوهسار + possessive ـم 'my mountain/height'; دو دیده is a nominative phrase 'two eyes' (poetic way to say 'my two eyes' or 'I watched'); به راه is a locative 'on/along the road' expressing the object of watching.
segments.s613-g198: بدان تا چه فرمان دهد پیشگاه = 'to that end / so as to know what command he will give before the court.' بدَان here is the fused infinitive/imperative notion 'to know/see' (ب + دان), introducing the purpose clause; تا marks the purpose 'so that/in order that'; چه is the interrogative 'what'; فرمان دهد is a subordinate clause with دهد (present/future sense 'will give'); پیشگاه is the locative 'before the throne / in the royal presence.'
segments.s613-g199: ز من باد بر شاه ایران درود = 'May there be (lit. may it be) my greeting upon the king of Iran.' ز (poetic 'from/of') + من 'my' mark the possessor of the greeting; باد is the optative particle 'may (it be)'; بر شاه ایران 'upon the king of Iran' uses بر as the preposition 'upon/on'; درود is the noun 'greeting/blessing.' The whole is an optative formula (wish/blessing).
segments.s613-g200: بمان زنده تا نام تارست و پود = 'Remain alive until name, warp and weft (endure).' بمان is the imperative/optative 'remain/stay'; زنده 'alive' is the predicative complement; تا introduces the durational clause 'until'; نام here means 'name/reputation (fame)'; تارست and پود are archaisms — پود is the familiar 'weft' (weaving), while تارست is an older/obscure term related to textile imagery (so the phrase evokes 'as long as the name is woven into warp and weft' i.e. as long as his renown endures). Grammatically the clause is a wishing/imprecatory durative: 'may he live until…'.
segments.s613-g49: گر introduces a conditional ('if'); افگندنی is a verbal-adjective/infinitive meaning 'to be cast/thrown' and is the predicate modified by هیچ ('anything/any trivial thing') followed by بودی, the past-tense form indicating a counterfactual past ('had been'). مرا is the object clitic (me) plus the object marker را split into two tokens here, so the whole clause reads 'If there had been anything to cast away for me...'.
segments.s613-g50: مگر here works as an adversative/contrastive connector ('but/rather' or 'then'); مرد مهمان is the nominative subject ('the man, the guest'); ستودی is a past-tense verb 'praised' (third-person past); مرا را again marks the object 'me' (clitic + object marker). The clause reports the outcome/contrast: 'the guest praised me.'
segments.s613-g51: The construction نه ... و نه ... ('neither ... nor ...') negates two predicates. افگندنی هست literally 'is for casting/throwing' (افگندنی = 'to be thrown'; هست = copula), and خوردنی 'edible/to be eaten' is the second negative member; the verb/copula is present for the first predicate and understood for the second.
segments.s613-g52: Continuation of the same negative pairing: نه پوشیدنی ('not to be worn') و نه گستردنی ('nor to be spread/extended'). The copula is omitted here but understood from the previous clause, a common elliptical pattern.
segments.s613-g53: به جای دگر is a prepositional phrase 'to/at another place' (به = to/for, جای = place, دگر = other). خانه جویی is a noun+verbal-noun pair meaning 'seeking a house' (خانه = house, جویی = searching/seeking). رواست is the predicate 'it is fitting/permissible' (روا + ست copula), so the whole clause means 'it is proper to seek another house.'
segments.s613-g54: که here introduces a causal/explanatory clause ('for/because'); ایدر means 'here'; همه کارها ('all matters/affairs') is the domain; بی‌نواست is the predicate 'is wretched/destitute/helpless.' Together: 'for here, in all affairs, he is helpless.'
segments.s613-g55: ورا is a dative/locative adverbial 'to him' or 'thereunto' (here 'to him'). گفت usually would be 'said' (the token list's senses are off, so treated as the verb 'said'). بالش نگه کن is an imperative: بالش 'pillow' + نگه (root of نگه داشتن 'to hold/keep') + کن (imperative 'do'), so 'hold the pillow.' یکی here is an enclitic/softener meaning 'one/a little' or 'for a moment' attached to the command.
segments.s613-g56: که تا introduces a purpose/temporal clause ('so that / until'); تا برنشینم = 'until I sit up' (برنشینم = first-person subjunctive/present of 'to rise/sit up'); برو اندکی is a brief imperative 'go (away) a little'—here the speaker tells the addressee to step back a bit while he rises.
segments.s613-g33: بپرسید is a narrative past/imperative form from پرسیدن used here as 'asked' (3rd-person narrative). کاین is the archaic demonstrative 'this/that' placed before the noun. خانه ویران is the noun phrase 'ruined house'. کراست is an archaic/clitic form closing the question (functioning roughly like 'is whose' / 'whose is it'); it attaches to the clause to ask ownership or identity.
segments.s613-g34: میان begins a locative phrase 'between / among'. این جای ویران is 'this ruined place' (این modifies جای). چراست is the fused interrogative 'چرا است' = 'why is it?', so the clause asks about the location and the reason for the ruin; word order (میان + place + چراست) is archaic but standard in epic Persian.
segments.s613-g35: خداوند here is used as 'the lord/sovereign' and functions as the subject. گفت is the past-tense verb 'said'. این سرای منست is a predicative clause: این سرای = 'this house' and منست is an archaic contraction meaning 'is mine' (copula + possessive).
segments.s613-g36: همین is an emphatic 'this very / exactly'. بخت بد is the noun phrase 'bad fortune' or 'ill-luck'; رهنمای is a noun 'guide' used metaphorically. منست repeats the copular/possessive sense ('is mine' / 'belongs to me'), so the whole clause says the speaker attributes his condition or guidance to ill-fortune.
segments.s613-g37: The repeated نه ... نه ... structure lists absent items (neither ... nor ...). گاو normally 'ox/cattle' and ستم here works within the phrase to indicate hardship/privation; ایدر = این‌در (here) is a locative adverb. پوشش 'covering/shelter' and خر 'ass' are further items in the negative enumeration.
segments.s613-g38: Continuation of the negative list: نه دانش 'no learning/knowledge', نه مردی 'no manhood / bravery' (mردی can mean 'man' or 'manliness' — here an abstract quality), نه پای و نه پر 'neither feet nor feathers' (a poetic way to stress total desolation/no living things). The conjunction و links the final two items.
segments.s613-g39: مرا دیدی is 'you have seen me' (مرا = object marker + 'me', دیدی = 2nd-person past 'you saw'). اکنون 'now' frames the time. سرایم is 'my house' (سرای + possessive -م) and ببین is the imperative 'look' or 'see' addressed to the addressee, so the clause shifts from reporting sight to an instruction to look at the house.
segments.s613-g40: بدین here functions as 'with this / to this' (ب + دین, an archaic locution). خانه 'house' is the noun object. نفرین 'curse' is the head of the phrase. به از ... آفرین is a comparative idiom: 'better be cursed than praised' or 'a curse upon it rather than praise' — به از indicates preference/comparison and آفرین here means 'praise/acclamation'.
segments.s613-g249: بریشان is a contracted preposition + pronoun (بر + ایشان) meaning 'to/for them' and precedes the verb as its indirect object. ببخش is the second‑person imperative of بخشیدن ('grant/forgive'), so here an addressee is being asked to give or grant. این همه خواسته is a noun phrase ('these entire requests/wishes') with این (this/the present) modified by همه (all) and خواسته (the things asked for).
segments.s613-g250: برافروز is an imperative (addressing the same addressee as ببخش) meaning 'kindle/ignite/rekindle' (here metaphorical for rouse or revive). جان و روان is a coordinated noun phrase 'life (soul) and spirit' serving as the object of the verb; کاسته is an adjectival past participle 'diminished/lessened' modifying جان و روان (the ezāfe linking is omitted in poetic style).
segments.s613-g251: تو is the second‑person pronoun 'you'. با آنک ... سوی گنج is a prepositional phrase 'with that one who went toward the treasure'; آنک is the archaic relative 'that/who', and رفتی here functions as the past verb in the relative clause (an older poetic form of 'رفت' = 'went'). سوی is the directional preposition 'toward', گنج 'treasure', and the final باد is the optative particle 'may (it be)'.
segments.s613-g252: همه داد و پرهیزگاریت باد is a separate optative wish: همه داد 'all justice' (داد as abstract 'justice'), و 'and', پرهیزگاریت is 'your piety/piousness' formed from پرهیزگار with the 2nd‑person suffix -ت, and باد again is the optative 'may it be'. The clause is a benediction: 'may all justice and your piety be (with you)'.
segments.s613-g253: نهان کرده is the compound perfect/past participial construction 'has hidden' (نهان + کرده). دینار فرشیدورد is a noun + name sequence in which the name follows the noun as possessive/identifier — 'the dinars of Farshidvurd' (poetic order without an explicit ezāfe). The whole segment says that those dinars were hidden.
segments.s613-g254: بدو مان همی is an inverted, compressed clause meaning 'to him there remained a remnant indeed' (بدو = 'to him', مان = 'remnant', همی emphatic 'indeed/still'). تا introduces a result or purpose clause 'so that' and takes نماند ('may not remain' / 'would not be left') as the verb; به درد 'to/for pain' indicates what should not remain. In sum: a little was left to him so that no suffering would remain.
segments.s613-g255: مر is an archaic dative/ethical particle 'to me' (literally 'for me' / 'as for me'), followed by او را ('him' with the object marker را). چه ... چه ... is a correlative/exclamatory construction ('what ... and what ...' / 'whether ... or ...') listing items: دینار و گوهر 'dinar(s) and jewel(s)' and چه خاک 'or even dust' — effectively asking what value these things have for the speaker regarding that person.
segments.s613-g256: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. بایست here carries the meaning 'must/ought' and together with کردن (a light verb) forms an impersonal/verbless‑like construction meaning 'one must be made/laid' or 'it must be done'. همی is an emphatic/habitual particle. در مغاک means 'into the abyss/grave'. So the clause asks: when one must be placed in the pit (i.e. buried), what use are riches or jewels?
segments.s613-g177: همه کار گیتی is a noun phrase 'all the affairs of the world' with همه (indefinite/totalizing modifier) + کار (affair) + گیتی (world). به اندازه is an adverbial phrase 'to the proper measure / in due degree'; the final به at the end of this segment is a preposition that will attach to the next words (it governs 'دل شاه' in the following segment: به دل شاه = 'into / to the king's heart').
segments.s613-g178: دل شاه is the prepositional object governed by the preceding به ('into the king's heart'). ز اندیشه‌ها marks source or cause ('from/by thoughts'). تازه is a predicate adjective 'fresh/new' describing the state of the heart; the final به is poetic/elliptical here and together with تازه yields the sense 'became fresh / newly came' (a verbless or compressed verbal idea common in poetry: '[it] newly came/become').
segments.s613-g179: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain one / someone'; گم‌شده is a past-participle/adjectival 'lost / missing' describing that person; نام فرشیدورد is an appositive/identifier 'named Farshidvard' (نام used as 'with the name / called'). The whole phrase = 'a certain one, named Farshidvard, was missing.'
segments.s613-g180: نه ... و نه ... is the correlative negation 'neither ... nor ...'. در بزمگاه = 'in the banquet-hall / place of revelry'; اندر نبرد = 'in battle' (اندر is a poetic variant of در/درون meaning 'in'). Together: 'neither in the bāzmgāh nor in battle.'
segments.s613-g181: ندانست is the past negative of دانستن 'did not know' placed before the indefinite کس; in classical/poetic Persian the verb preceding کس yields the sense 'no one knew'. نام او is the object 'his name' and در جهان 'in the world' gives the domain. So: 'No one knew his name in the world.'
segments.s613-g182: میان ... و میان ... is a parallel, poetic 'among / between ... and ...'; کهان and مهان form a paired contrastive phrase (poetically 'heavens/lofty places' and 'guests/people' — i.e. everywhere/among all ranks). The repetition emphasizes universality: 'among the heavens and among the people.'
segments.s613-g183: نه ... و نه ... again negates two classes: خسروپرست is a compound meaning 'one who worships/kisses up to the king' (a sycophant), یزدان‌شناس 'one who knows God' (a theologian/pious person). The pair says 'neither the flatterers nor the pious persons...' (they both failed to do something).
segments.s613-g184: ندانست (did not know) + کردن (light verb) forms a construction 'did not know how to do (something)'; به چیزی سپاس is the verbal object 'to give any thanks' (سپاس = thanks, به چیزی = 'for anything' / 'any'). So the clause means 'did not know how to render any thanks.' Note that کردن is used as a light verb here to make سپاس into an act of giving thanks.
segments.s613-g81: کدیور بدو گفت is a straightforward S (name) + indirect object + V construction: the proper name (کدیور) is the subject, بدو (“to him”) is the prepositional/indirect-object phrase following the name, and گفت is the simple past 3rd-person verb “said.” زین در مرنج is an imperative/plea: زین is an archaic/deictic ‘of this/for this’ (classical contraction of از این), در here functions as a local/causal marker within the phrase, and مرنج is a negative imperative/subjunctive meaning “do not be upset.” Together it yields “do not be upset by this.”
segments.s613-g82: که introduces the content clause of گفت (“that...”). در خان من is locative: در = “in/inside,” خان = “khān/inn/caravanserai,” and من is the possessive pronoun placed after the noun (‘my khān’). کس نیابد is a negative indefinite construction: کس here functions as “no one” when combined with the negative verb نیابد (3rd-person present/subjunctive/negative form ‘does not find’), and سپنج is the direct object placed at the end (SOV order), meaning “sponge.”
segments.s613-g83: بدو گفت again follows the pattern ‘(he) said to him.’ شاه‌ای is a vocative: شاه + the vocative particle ای (“O king”), and the adjectives خردمند پیر follow the noun as is normal in Persian (noun + adjectives), meaning “wise, old.” The phrase addresses the addressee with two epithets.
segments.s613-g84: چه باشی is a rhetorical question using چه (“what/why”) plus باشی, here a present-subjunctive/optative form meaning roughly “why are you/what are you doing.” به پیشم = “before me/in my presence” (به + پیشم). همی is an older verbal particle adding continuity or emphasis (“still/so”), خیره is an adjective/participle “staring/astounded,” and خیر is an interjection/particle here expressing surprise or ‘what for’—the whole string asks why the addressee stands before the speaker, staring.
segments.s613-g85: چنانچون introduces a similative clause “just as/like,” and گمانم is the noun گمان with the possessive enclitic -م (‘my belief/suspicion’). هم adds ‘also/emphatically,’ and از + آب سرد marks origin or cause: از = “from/because of,” آب سرد = “cold water” (noun then adjective). The whole phrase means “as I thought, (because) of the cold water...”
segments.s613-g86: ببخشای is an archaic/polite 2nd-person imperative or optative meaning “forgive” / “pardon me.” ای is the vocative marker (“O!”) and مرد آزادمرد is a doubled vocative: مرد (“man/hero”) followed by the compound آزادمرد (“free/noble man”), addressed respectfully to the addressee. The sequence is a polite plea: “Pardon me, O noble man.”
segments.s613-g87: کدیور بدو گفت repeats the reported-speech frame (subject + indirect object + past verb). کان here functions as a demonstrative/classical particle “that” (introducing the thing being pointed out) and آبگیر is the noun “reservoir/pond.” The phrase simply names the object pointed out in the reported clause.
segments.s613-g88: به پیش است places the location before the copula: به پیش = “in front/nearby,” است = present copula “is.” کمتر ز پرتاب تیر is a comparative phrase: کمتر = “less/shorter,” ز is the poetic preposition “than/from,” and پرتاب تیر is a nominal phrase “the throw/flight of an arrow” (پرتاب + تیر). Together: “it is before us, at a distance less than an arrow’s throw.”
segments.s613-g57: بدو گفت is literal word order 'to him he said' — بدو is the dative clitic/phrase 'to him' placed before the verb, گفت is the reporting verb 'said'. ایدر (archaic for اینجا) + نه + جای + نکوست = 'here not place is-good' with the copula omitted or folded into نکوست; the نه negates 'نکوست' so the whole phrase means 'this is not a good place.'
segments.s613-g58: همانا is an emphatic particle 'truly/indeed'. ترا is the object clitic (ت + را written together here) meaning 'you/to you'. شیر مرغ is a compound nominal 'lion-bird' (a mythical or figurative creature) serving as the subject, and آرزوست is a nominal predicate 'is a longing/desire' (copula - است is fused into the poetic form).
segments.s613-g59: پس‌انگاه گفتش queuing the report 'then he said to him' (گفتش = گفت + ش, the -ش enclitic = 'to him'). که introduces the reported clause. شیر آر گرم is a nominal phrase where شیر آر = 'the longing for the lion' (شیر = lion, آر = desire) and گرم (here adjectival) = 'ardent/fervent' — the copula is omitted, typical in poetry.
segments.s613-g60: چنان چون together act like 'just as / when'. بیابی is a present/subjunctive form of یافتن/یابیدن used here in a protasis ('when/if you find') rather than a simple indicative. یکی نان نرم = 'one/ a single soft bread' with یکی before the noun and the adjective نرم after it, following poetic word order.
segments.s613-g61: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed phrase 'thus gave an answer' with داد functioning as the past-tense verb 'gave'. که introduces the content of his reply; ایدو is an archaic/poetic temporal/ demonstrative adverb here meaning roughly 'then'/'so', and گمان is used as 'think/assume' or 'suppose' in reported speech.
segments.s613-g62: This is the content of the reply: که (that) + خوردی 'you ate' (past 2nd sg). و گشتی is 'and you became' — گشتی is a past form meaning 'became/turned' (here used as 'became'), and ازو شادمان = 'happy from/with it' (ازو = از او = 'because of it/of that', شادمان = 'happy'). Note: some listed lemma senses do not include the verbal senses used here, so those are indicated as unavailable.
segments.s613-g63: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. نان is the object 'bread'. بدی here is an archaic/poetic form used as 'you give' (the verb دادن); در تنم 'in my body' (در + تنم with ـم possessive 'my') and جان بدی 'you give life' — the clause means 'if you give bread, you give life into my body.' Several of the listed lemma senses do not match these verbal/possessive usages, so the corresponding sense indices are marked unavailable.
segments.s613-g64: اگر چند here functions idiomatically as 'even though / although'. جانم is 'my life' (جان + ـم possessive). به از is a comparative idiom meaning 'better than / worthier than' (به + از), so جانم به از نان بدی = 'though my life is worth more than bread.' بدی again is the verbal 'you give' (here part of the comparative clause). Some provided lemma senses do not reflect these classical/poetic uses (possession, idiomatic comparison, verbal form), so they are marked accordingly.
