segments.s613-g145: بدان is here composed of the prepositional prefix ب- (meaning “to”) attached to دان/آن (’that’), so the clause literally begins “to that (man) he said.” ز ایدر برو is an imperative instruction: ز = “from,” ایدر = “here,” and برو = “go”; word order places the source (ز ایدر) before the verb برو.
segments.s613-g146: همی خارکندی is a habitual/past-habit construction: همی + verb-stem indicates a repeated or customary action (‘you used to pull out thorns’). کنون = “now” shifts time to the present, and زر درو is an imperative-like or directive phrase meaning “reap/gather the gold” (درو = harvest/reap). The sentence moves from addressing the man to contrasting his former work with the new task.
segments.s613-g147: ازان خواسته ده یکی is an imperative clause: ازان خواسته = “of/from that request,” ده = imperative ‘give,’ and یکی = “one” (the object to give). مر تراست literally reads مر (an older form of 'mara' = ‘me/to me’) + تراست (تَرا است = ‘it is yours’), a terse idiomatic phrasing meaning roughly ‘it belongs to you’ (the exact possessive nuance is compacted in older usage).
segments.s613-g148: بدین = به + این (to these) introduces the indirect object; مردمان = “the people”; راه = “way/path”; بنمای is the imperative of نمایاندن ‘show’ (بنمای = ‘show!’) and راست here qualifies the manner (‘right/true’ or ‘straight’), so the clause is the direct command “show these people the right way.” Note the preposition + demonstrative contraction in بدین.
segments.s613-g149: دل افرزو is an epithet (دل‌افروز, literally ‘heart‑brightening’/a personal epithet), followed by بد نام = ‘ill‑famed’ or ‘of bad reputation’; آن خارزن = ‘that thorn-clearer’ (demonstrative + agent). The adjective sequence places the epithet and reputation before the noun it describes.
segments.s613-g150: گرازنده مردی = ‘a boar‑hunter of a man’ i.e. ‘a man who hunted boars’; به نیروی تن is an ablative/instrumental phrase ‘by/with the strength of his body’ (به + نیروی + تن). The preposition به here has the sense ‘by/with’ introducing the means.
segments.s613-g151: گرانمایه اسپی is an adjective + noun order common in older Persian: ‘a precious/valued steed’ (اسپی = archaic form of اسب). بدو داد = ‘gave to him’ (بدو = to him, داد = gave). The clause ends with و گفت = ‘and he said,’ introducing reported speech.
segments.s613-g152: This clause reports speech with two subordinators که…که: the first که = ‘that,’ با باد = ‘with the wind’ (i.e. swiftly), باید = modal ‘must/ought,’ and the following که introduces the clause containing گردی تو جفت. گردی is here a verbal/noun-form meaning ‘to roam/ to go about’ or ‘to move,’ while تو جفت = ‘you (are) a match/pair’ — the sense is idiomatic: ‘you must run with the wind; you are its equal/match.’ Word order places the modal باید before the verb phrase and finishes with the predicative جفت.
segments.s613-g113: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (poetic past), and مهتر is the speaker 'the chief/elder' (subject). بدین = به + این ('to/into this') modifies شارستان 'town/city' and sits after the verb; the whole order is somewhat inverted (Prepositional object + Verb + Subject + locative).
segments.s613-g114: کرا is the archaic interrogative 'who/whom'; دانی is 2nd-person singular present 'you deem/consider' (literally 'do you know/consider'); ای is the vocative particle 'O', and دشمن خارستان is the vocative phrase 'enemy of the thorn-plain/thicket'. The clause functions as a direct question addressed to the addressee.
segments.s613-g115: چنین 'thus' introduces the manner; داد is the past 'gave' (he gave an answer), and پاسخ is the noun 'answer/reply'. که begins the subordinate clause reporting the content of that answer; فرشیدورد is the personal name (logical subject of the following clause).
segments.s613-g116: بماند is a third-person present/jussive form of ماندن ('may/should remain' or 'would remain') dependent on the reporting clause introduced by که; همه‌ساله 'yearly/for every year' is an adverbial phrase modifying بماند; بی‌خواب و خورد is a coordinated description of his state — 'sleepless and خورد' (خورد here is the verb 'to eat' used poetically, so 'eating' or by extension 'consumed/gnawed at'), describing what he will be during those years.
segments.s613-g117: مگر introduces an exception or contrast here ('except/except that'); گوسفندش is 'his sheep' (possessive suffix -ش = his), بود is the past copula 'were/was', and صدهزار is the numeral predicate 'one hundred thousand' following the copula — typical predicate-number order with بود.
segments.s613-g118: همان is a demonstrative 'that same' (referring back to the number just stated); اسپ و استر are two coordinated nouns 'horse and mare'; بود is the copula 'were'; زین شمار is an archaic prepositional/partitive phrase 'of this number' (زین = از این 'of/from this') specifying that those horses and mares make up that count.
segments.s613-g119: زمین 'the land/earth' is the topic; پر ز آگنده دینار 'full (پر) from/of, piled with (آگنده) dinars' is the adjectival/participle phrase describing it, and اوست (او + ست) places possession: 'they are his' or 'it is his'. Word order places the place/topic first and the predicate (full of coins and belonging to him) after.
segments.s613-g120: که introduces a result/extent clause: 'such that...'. مه here is the moon (a poetic image), مغز 'core/innermost part', بادش is باد + -ش 'his wind/fortune' (here figuratively his powerful influence or wealth), بتن‌بر is an archaic or compound word (literally 'idol-destroyer' in the lemma list) used here as a strong transitive image 'would strip/tear away', and مه پوست repeats the moon image 'the moon's skin' — the clause is hyperbolic: by his force/fortune even the moon's core and skin would be affected. Note the compact, elliptical poetic word order and archaic compounds.
segments.s613-g73: به زر تیغ داری: 'به' is instrumental/associative here ('with/by'), followed by 'زر' (gold) modifying 'تیغ' (sword). 'داری' is the 2nd‑person singular present of داشتن, 'you have'. The repeated 'به' before 'زربر رکیب' again marks possession/association: 'زربر' is an adjective 'gilded/gold‑covered' modifying 'رکیب' ('companion, rider, or mount'); the whole phrase lists possessions that set up the contrast in the next clause.
segments.s613-g74: نباید که آید ز دزدت نهیب: 'نباید' is an impersonal/modal negative ('it ought not / one should not'); 'که' introduces the subordinate clause. 'آید' (from آمدن) is present/subjunctive-ish 'comes' or 'should come'; 'ز' = 'from'; 'دزدت' = 'your thief' (دزد + enclitic -ت). 'نهیب' is a noun 'a loud cry / shout' (or 'rebuke'). The clause literally states 'it should not be that a cry comes from your thief', i.e. warns not to assume you will not hear a thief's shout.
segments.s613-g75: ز یزدان بترس و ز من دور باش: 'ز' = 'from' (poetic). 'یزدان' = God; 'بترس' is the 2nd‑person singular imperative 'fear!'. The conjunction 'و' links the second imperative: 'ز من دور باش' = 'be distant from me' (stay away). Both verbs are imperatives giving commands/advice.
segments.s613-g76: به هر کار چون من تو رنجور باش: 'به هر کار' = 'in/for every affair' (به + هر + کار). 'چون من' = 'like me' (comparison phrase). Word order places the comparison before the person addressed: 'تو' (you) + 'رنجور' (adjectival predicate 'distressed/sickly') + 'باش' imperative 'be'. The clause urges the addressee to emulate the speaker's afflicted state in all matters.
segments.s613-g77: چو خانه برین‌گونه ویران بود: 'چو' here = 'when'. 'خانه' is subject 'the house'. 'برین‌گونه' is an adverbial phrase 'in this manner/thus'. 'ویران' is predicate adjective 'ruined' and 'بود' is the past copula 'was'. Together they form a temporal clause 'When the house was thus ruined...'.
segments.s613-g78: گذرگاه دزدان و شیران بود: 'گذرگاه' is the subject 'the passage/way'; the following genitive phrase ('دزدان و شیران') indicates who frequented it — 'thieves and شیران' (شیران can mean 'lions' or metaphorically 'brave men'); 'بود' is the past 'was'. The noun phrase describes the passage as a haunt/breeding ground of dangerous creatures/people.
segments.s613-g79: بدو گفت اگر دزد شمشیر من: 'بدو گفت' = 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto it', گفت = 'said'—classical word order put the datives before the verb). 'اگر' introduces the conditional 'if'; 'دزد' is the conditional subject 'the thief'; 'شمشیر من' = 'my sword' (possession). This is the protasis (if‑clause) of a conditional sentence whose apodosis follows.
segments.s613-g80: ببردی کنون نیستی زیر من: 'ببردی' is a past/perfect form 'you (or he) took/has taken away' (here completing the conditional: 'if the thief has taken my sword'). 'کنون' = 'now'; 'نیستی' is 2nd‑person present 'you are not'; 'زیر من' literally 'under me' meaning 'subject to me' or 'under my protection/control'. The apodosis states the result: if that happened, you are no longer under my authority/protection now.
segments.s613-g233: The clause is negative: 'کسی ... نیست' = 'no one ... is/exists.' 'زین بزرگان' is a genitive phrase meaning 'of these great ones' (archaic زین = از این). 'پدیدار' functions as a predicative adjective/verb 'manifest/visible; appear', and 'نیست' negates that state.
segments.s613-g234: The second clause states there is no fighting with the ruler: 'با خداوند پیکار نیست' = 'there is no battle with the lord/sovereign.' 'بدین' is an archaic/compound form here and functions adverbially/locatively (sense not listed in the provided options); 'پیکار' is the noun 'battle' and 'نیست' negates its existence. Note that 'خداوند' here means 'sovereign/king' rather than the theological God.
segments.s613-g235: 'تو' is the vocative/subject 'you.' 'آن خواسته' = 'that requested thing' or 'that which is wished for.' 'گرد کن' is an imperative formed from the stem 'گرد' (go about/search) plus the auxiliary/imperative 'کن' = 'do' — together 'go seek.' 'هرچ هست' = 'whatever it is' (an indefinite qualifier).
segments.s613-g236: 'ببخش' is an imperative meaning 'give/grant (it).' 'و' links clauses. 'مبر' is the negative imperative 'do not take.' 'زان' = 'from that/it.' The phrase 'به یک چیز دست' literally 'a hand to one thing' is idiomatic: 'do not even lay a hand on a single thing' — i.e. 'do not touch/withhold even one item.'
segments.s613-g237: 'کسی را که' introduces a relative/indefinite clause 'whoever / the one who' with 'را' giving emphasis/definiteness to the person. 'پوشیده دارد' = 'keeps (it) hidden' (پوشیده adjective + دارد 'has/keeps'). 'نیاز' is the object 'need/necessity/supplication.'
segments.s613-g238: The clause explains the consequence: 'که از بد همی دیر یابد جواز' — 'که' connects the clause, 'از بد' = 'because of misfortune/badness,' 'همی' is an archaic present/future particle often adding emphasis, 'دیر یابد' literally 'finds late' = 'scarcely/with difficulty obtains,' and 'جواز' = 'permission/allowance.'
segments.s613-g239: 'همان نیز' ties back to what was just said ('that same / likewise'). 'پیری' here = 'old man / old age' (a person aged), 'که' introduces the relative clause 'who/when,' and 'بیکار گشت' is a past/result clause 'became idle' (گشت = became).
segments.s613-g240: 'به چشم' = 'in the sight/eyes of,' 'گرانمایگان' = 'the illustrious/noble persons,' and 'خوار گشت' = 'became despised' (خوار = despised, گشت = became). The whole phrase says he came to be looked down on by the great.
segments.s613-g17: که is a relative pronoun 'which/that' linking back to something mentioned earlier. بردی is 2nd-person singular past 'you carried/took'. به خروار is a measure phrase: به + خروار = 'in the amount/quantity of a khrōvār' i.e. 'in great quantities.' تا here functions as a directional 'as far as/to', and خان خویش = 'his/one's own khan/house' (خویش is the reflexive possessive formed from خوی + ش).
segments.s613-g18: بر is a preposition indicating beneficiary/recipient here ('for' / 'to'). کودک خرد is a noun + adjective ('little/young child' — خرد used as 'small'). و links the two recipients, and مهمان خویش = 'his own guest' (خویش as the reflexive possessive clitic). The whole phrase gives the people for whom the goods were carried.
segments.s613-g19: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. ماهی here is a poetic/figurative word (a beautiful luminous body — often the moon or a beautiful face) and برآمد is the past 'rose/appeared'. شتاب آمدش = 'haste/hastefulness came upon him' with the clitic -ش indicating 'to him.' The clause describes an event occurring at dawn/when the moon rose.
segments.s613-g20: همی is an emphatic/continuous particle (here marking an event that happened). با = 'with'. بتان (plural of بت) is used poetically for 'the beauties' (or idols/beautiful ones). رای here means 'thought/plan/idea' and functions with بتان to indicate the content or colouring of the vision, and خواب is 'sleep' or 'a dream' that came to him (آمدش = 'came to him'). Altogether: a dream/sleep arrived, accompanied by images or thoughts of the beauties.
segments.s613-g21: بیاورد is 3rd-person singular past 'he brought' (classical/perfective form). لشکر is the direct object 'an army'. ز + نخچیرگاه = 'from the hunting-ground' (ز is the poetic preposition 'from'). Word order places the verb before its object, which is common in epic verse.
segments.s613-g22: ز here has a locative/partitive sense 'around/from among', and گرد سواران = 'around the horsemen' (گرد = around/about; سواران = riders/horsemen). ندیدند is 3rd-person plural past 'they did not see', and راه is the object 'the road/way' — so 'the riders around did not see the road.'
segments.s613-g23: همی is a particle indicating continued or habitual action ('kept on'). رفت is 3rd-person past 'went'. لشکر is the subject 'the army'. به کردار گرد is a prepositional phrase of manner: به + کردار ('in the manner/way') and گرد 'around/in circles' — 'the army kept moving in a circular way.'
segments.s613-g24: چنین = 'thus/so'. تا = 'until'. رخ روز is a poetic compound 'the face of day' (i.e. daylight/dawn). شد is past 'became', and لاژورد is a color noun 'lapis-lazuli' used for an intense blue — 'became lapis-blue' describes the sky at daybreak.

sentences.s613-s129: May the directing heavens be your companion; may that justice and restraint be your task.
sentences.s613-s130: They affixed the king's seal to the courier; he sent (him), returned, and came on the road.
sentences.s613-s65: Thus the king said to the kharzan: "If you do not know the number of his sheep..."
sentences.s613-s66: Know, truly, where he keeps it; his comptroller (or treasurer) told you who his companion is.
sentences.s613-s67: Thus he said, 'O battle‑seasoned horseman, no one knows how many he has sought from that.'
sentences.s613-s68: He gave that kharzan a few dinars, and said to him, 'Now you have become honorable.'
sentences.s613-s21: He dismounted from his horse and saw that dwelling; the sovereign's hands and feet became weak.
sentences.s613-s22: The whole house was fouled with sheep dung; one vaulted arch stood on a base and on a high place.
sentences.s613-s23: He said to him, 'Bring something to sit on; hoist it up, O hospitable man.'
sentences.s613-s24: He answered thus: 'Why do you stare in amazement and laugh at the host, O marzban?'
sentences.s613-s45: Eat as much as you wish, and take (some) as well; what are you looking for in this poor house?
sentences.s613-s46: Truly, you saw a dervish-man, a man brought low by old age and worn out from his labors.
sentences.s613-s47: He answered thus: 'If you lack superiority, do not wage war with an army.'
sentences.s613-s48: Farshidvard said to him, 'What is your name? I have neither home nor cloak, neither sleep nor food.'
sentences.s613-s113: We will not build a treasure's foundation from that hardship; we will not lock our heart inside a sponge-house.
sentences.s613-s114: Fereydun is no longer seen in the world; those same Irāj, Salm and Tur are among the great.
sentences.s613-s115: Those same Jam and Kaous together with Kay Qobad — apart from these renowned men of whom we keep remembrance.
sentences.s613-s116: My father — he whose heart was full of pain — was not a just man; he was dishonourable.
sentences.s613-s53: He said this and wept so piteously that the king fled from his voice.
sentences.s613-s54: He laughed at that old man and set out on the road; his army followed him constantly.
sentences.s613-s55: When he went out of the renowned city, a certain thorny thicket came up before him.
sentences.s613-s56: A man held an axe and was cutting thorns; from the army the king came forward toward him.
sentences.s613-s121: All his other possessions were consumed, and now he remains with pain and the chill wind.
sentences.s613-s122: One who has neither a name nor dinars finds no partner in commerce.
sentences.s613-s123: There are other children you see who are orphans — their father dead and left without gold and silver.
sentences.s613-s124: Women who are without husbands and unprotected, who know no craft and make no effort.
sentences.s613-s61: His belly remained hungry, his body naked; he had neither children nor kin, nor goods or baggage.
sentences.s613-s62: If he sold his slayer for gold, he would make one house of his homeland full of jewels.
sentences.s613-s63: His shepherds would boil meat in milk; he himself ate millet bread with cheese.
sentences.s613-s64: He had never seen two garments together; therefore hardship was also upon his body.
